one_line_takeaway: MM-AttacKG is the first LLM pipeline to mine images in CTI reports for attack-graph construction, adding a brainstorm-extract-verify-integrate loop on top of AttacKG+ that lifts entity/relation/technique F-1 above text-only and ICL/CoT baselines but leaves technique identification and cross-modal reasoning unsolved.
key_finding: Adding parsed image information improves threat extraction over text-only methods, and MM-AttacKG beats the ICL and CoT multimodal prompting baselines on F-1 across all three subtasks. In Table 2, MM-AttacKG reaches Entity F-1 0.7716, Relation F-1 0.8147, and Technique F-1 0.5703, versus the best text baseline AttacKG+ at 0.6274 / 0.7222 / 0.4491 and versus ICL (0.7107 / 0.7640 / 0.5156) and CoT (0.7105 / 0.7599 / 0.5277). Technique identification is the hardest subtask for every method (all F-1s well below entity/relation). Ablations show both Brainstorming and Verification matter: removing both (w/o B&V) cuts Entity F-1 to 0.6749 and Relation F-1 to 0.7917, though it slightly raises entity precision, revealing a precision/recall trade-off. Brainstorming generates ~21 questions per image; of 1,281 initially generated questions, filtering keeps 47.0% direct-correlation and 41.9% answer-oriented while discarding 11.2% (143) as non-related. The Stuxnet case study extracts 6 tactical stages and 14 techniques from text, then image integration surfaces 3 additional techniques (T1003 OS Credential Dumping, T1107 Function Hooking, T1546 Event Triggered Execution).
core_contribution: MM-AttacKG is a framework that, for the first time (per the authors), incorporates the images embedded in Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports ('threat images': attack-flow diagrams, charts, code screenshots, tables, timelines, etc.) into LLM-based Attack Graph Construction (AGC), which prior work builds purely from text. The paper's own novel contribution is a five-module pipeline layered on top of an existing text constructor (AttacKG+, which is the authors' own prior work [12] and is used as-is, not a contribution of this paper): (1) Text-based Construction via AttacKG+ producing quintuple atomic events (subject, action, object, timestamp, TTP label) from CTI text; (2) Brainstorming, which seeds an MLLM with 'leading questions' by image type and generates a pool of general + task-specific questions per image; (3) Extraction, which answers those questions using two kinds of parsing-support context (Image-Aware-Context from the image's surrounding paragraph, and Global-Context from the report abstract); (4) Verification, a two-stage quality control combining question filtering (direct-correlation vs. answer-oriented capture) and answer refinement via two self-iterating prompt paradigms (Question-led and Answer-Iteration) scored on accuracy/consistency/completeness/relevance; and (5) Integration, which fuses the image-derived 'threat enhancement references' into the text attack graph through Node Extension, Relation Update, and Technique Addition (MITRE ATT&CK). A secondary contribution is the AG-LLM-mm multimodal CTI dataset.
threat_model: This is a defensive CTI-analysis / tooling paper, not an attack paper. The 'threats' it reasons about are the real-world cyber attacks described in analyst CTI reports (e.g., APT campaigns, Stuxnet); the system's job is to reconstruct those attacks as structured attack graphs for defenders. The paper does NOT posit an adversary acting against MM-AttacKG itself (no adversarial-robustness, poisoning, or evasion threat model against the pipeline is claimed or evaluated). It briefly cites multimodal-LLM security work (backdoors, poisoning, jailbreaks) only in Related Work, not as its own contribution. So the operative 'model' is: given inherently-multimodal CTI reports, extract more complete threat entities/relations/techniques than text-only methods.
limitations: Scope and rigor caveats the authors and text expose: (1) Modalities are parsed separately rather than through end-to-end cross-modal reasoning/cross-verification (flagged in Future Work). (2) Analysis is per single report; no multi-source/joint threat-report correlation. (3) Relies on general-purpose Qwen2.5-VL without domain-specific pretraining, which the authors say leaves gaps on complex security problems. (4) Technique identification remains weak in absolute terms (best F-1 ~0.57), so image gains do not fix the hardest subtask. (5) AG-LLM-mm is described as 'exploratory' and small (question analyses use random samples of ~10 images; only 2 vendor sources), raising external-validity questions, and code + dataset are only 'released upon acceptance' (not yet available), limiting reproducibility. (6) Evaluation of answer quality and much of the ground truth relies on LLM self-scoring plus a handful of graduate-student annotators, i.e., no independent large-scale gold standard. (7) The text backbone AttacKG+ is the authors' own prior work, so gains are measured relative to their own pipeline rather than diverse external constructors.

datasets_used: AG-LLM-mm (authors' new multimodal CTI dataset built from Cisco Talos Intelligence Group and Microsoft Security Intelligence Center reports; text split via AttacKG+, images extracted via MinerU-PDFScanner)
quantitative_results: MM-AttacKG Entity extraction F-1 = 0.7716 (Precision 0.7224, Recall 0.8280), Table 2
MM-AttacKG Relation extraction F-1 = 0.8147 (Precision 0.7460, Recall 0.8973), Table 2
MM-AttacKG Technique identification F-1 = 0.5703 (Precision 0.5256, Recall 0.6232), Table 2
Best text baseline AttacKG+ F-1: Entity 0.6274, Relation 0.7222, Technique 0.4491, Table 2
ICL image-enhanced F-1: Entity 0.7107, Relation 0.7640, Technique 0.5156; CoT F-1: 0.7105 / 0.7599 / 0.5277, Table 2
Ablation w/o Brainstorming&Verification: Entity F-1 drops to 0.6749, Relation 0.7917, Technique 0.5386, Table 3
Brainstorming generates ~21 questions per CTI image; 1,281 questions generated total during dataset construction
Question filtering distribution: direct-correlation 0.4699 (602), answer-oriented 0.4185 (536), non-related 0.1116 (143), Table 4
Stuxnet case study: 6 tactical stages and 14 techniques from text; image integration adds 3 new techniques (T1003, T1107, T1546)
Implementation: Qwen2.5-VL-72B/32B/7B, temperature 0.7 with fixed seed, 512-token output cap for image parsing
baselines_compared: AttacKG+ [text-based, authors' prior work, also the text backbone]
